Short answer
- Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service: it indexes a customer’s documentation and returns highly compressed, highly relevant context for use by the customer’s own LLM—reducing large document sets to a small, token-budgeted context so the customer’s LLM can produce accurate responses.

Supporting facts from the documentation
- "90% Context Reduction: From 500K tokens to ~5K relevant tokens." (Solution: Context Optimization as a Service)
- It "indexes documentation and returns optimized context to LLM clients via the Model Context Protocol (MCP)." (Project Overview)
- Key benefits called out: token budgeting (fit content to client-specified max_tokens), LLM-agnostic (works with Claude, GPT, Gemini, local models), no LLM costs (Snipara does not run inference; clients use their own LLMs), session context persistence, zero setup, and team/shared documentation support. (Solution: Context Optimization as a Service; Product Philosophy)
- Explicit "Key Insight": "The real value of RLM is not the LLM response—it's the context optimization that makes the LLM response possible within token limits." (Product Philosophy)

These points together define Snipara’s core proposition: deliver dramatically reduced, highly relevant documentation context so clients can get better LLM results without vendor lock‑in or added inference costs.

Snipara’s core value proposition (from the docs):

- Context Optimization as a Service — Snipara MCP Server indexes documentation and returns an optimized set of context for LLM clients (not LLM inference).
- 90% context reduction — compresses large docs (example: from ~500K tokens) into ~5K highly relevant tokens.
- Token budgeting — fits returned context to the client’s specified max_tokens.
- LLM-agnostic / client-run inference — clients use their own LLM (no vendor lock-in; Snipara does not run LLM inference).
- No LLM costs passed through — Snipara charges for context optimization only (compute), not for LLM inference.
- Practical features that support the value: session context persistence, zero setup hosted MCP endpoint, and team collaboration for shared indexed docs.

Key insight (documented): the real value is the optimized context that enables effective LLM responses within token limits.

Summary — purpose
- Snipara's token budgeting picks the most relevant document content that fits a caller's token budget so an LLM receives a small, high-quality context (e.g., “the right 4K tokens out of 500K”).

Key components (tools & libs)
- Primary tool: rlm_context_query (apps/mcp-server/src/rlm_engine.py).
- Token counting: tiktoken using the cl100k_base encoder (pyproject requirement tiktoken>=0.5.0).
- Parameters exposed: query, max_tokens (default 4000), search_mode (keyword|semantic|hybrid), include_metadata.

End-to-end flow
1. Search: engine does keyword, semantic, or hybrid search to produce candidate sections.
2. Rank: candidates are ranked by relevance.
3. Token-aware selection:
   - Each candidate's token count is measured with cl100k_base.
   - Greedy selection: pick highest-relevance sections first.
   - Stop when adding the next full section would exceed max_tokens.
   - If a partial section can fit, it is truncated (smart_truncate) to exactly fill remaining tokens and marked truncated.
4. Return: the tool returns selected sections plus metadata and total_tokens; client passes that context to their LLM.

Smart truncation behavior
- Truncation tries not to cut mid-sentence: it truncates at sentence boundaries (., !, ?), appends "..." and sets truncated=true in section metadata.

Metadata returned for each section
- title, content (possibly truncated), file path, line range, relevance_score, token_count, truncated flag (when applicable).
- Response also includes total_tokens, truncated (overall), session_context (if available), and suggestions.

Budget-allocation policies (documented strategies)
- Category-based allocations example (Token Budget Algorithm):
  - MANDATORY: 40%
  - BEST_PRACTICES: 30%
  - GUIDELINES: 20%
  - REFERENCE: 10%
  - Each category has a priority ordering used when filling budgets.
- A web-service allocation variant (DEFAULT_ALLOCATION) prioritizes project content first:
  - project: 40%
  - MANDATORY: 25%
  - BEST_PRACTICES: 20%
  - GUIDELINES: 10%
  - REFERENCE: 5%
- Allocation is done per-category: documents are sorted by relevance and included until the category budget is exhausted; documents that would exceed the category budget are marked unallocated.

Other documented details
- Session context can be included (notes indicate it may be limited to a fraction of the budget, e.g., up to 20%).
- System instructions are auto-injected by the engine.
- The implementation purposely returns file/line metadata and token counts so the returned context is auditable and citable.
- Snipara claims token-optimization benefits in the docs (e.g., large reductions such as “90% reduction” are listed in the feature comparison).

Files / implementation pointers (where this is implemented)
- rlm_context_query implementation and token-budget logic in apps/mcp-server/src/rlm_engine.py.
- Pydantic schemas added in models.py (ContextQueryParams, ContextSection, ContextQueryResult).
- Token-budget allocation logic examples in web code (apps/web/lib/services/token-budget.ts).

Here’s how Snipara’s token-budgeting works, based on the provided documentation:

Core mechanism
- Token counting uses tiktoken with the cl100k_base encoder (GPT-4/Claude compatible). Sections’ token sizes are computed by encoding their text.
- Context selection is greedy by relevance: candidate sections are ranked, and the engine adds the highest‑relevance sections until the token budget would be exceeded.
- If a full section would exceed the remaining budget, a partial section may be included by smart_truncate() to fit the remaining tokens (see Smart truncation below).
- The context query tool exposing this behavior is rlm_context_query (params described below); the tool returns selected sections, per‑section metadata, total tokens used, and flags about truncation.

rlm_context_query (interface & output)
- Request parameters (from docs): query (string), max_tokens (int, default 4000), search_mode ("keyword" | "semantic" | "hybrid"), include_metadata (bool).
- Response includes: sections (each with title, content, file path, line range, relevance_score, token_count, and truncated flag when applicable), total_tokens, truncated (boolean), session_context, suggestions.
- Metadata returned per section includes file path, line ranges, relevance score, and token counts.

Smart truncation
- When partial content must be used to fit the remaining budget, smart_truncate() truncates at sentence boundaries (cut on . ! ?), appends an ellipsis ("..."), and marks the section as truncated.
- The overall response sets truncated = true when the total selected content reached the budget.

Session context
- Session context can be included in the returned context; implementation notes specify session context inclusion may be limited (documentation notes “up to 20% of budget”).

Budget allocation strategies (shared vs project docs)
- There are category-based allocation presets used by the token-budgeting/allocation logic:
  - One snippet defines CATEGORY_BUDGETS for shared categories: MANDATORY 0.4, BEST_PRACTICES 0.3, GUIDELINES 0.2, REFERENCE 0.1.
  - The budget-allocation logic used in the web service gives project docs priority and a DEFAULT_ALLOCATION that looks like: project 0.40, MANDATORY 0.25, BEST_PRACTICES 0.20, GUIDELINES 0.10, REFERENCE 0.05.
- Allocation code: project documents are allocated first (using their portion of the budget), then shared docs are allocated by category using the configured percentages. Within a category, docs are sorted by relevance and added until that category’s budget is exhausted.

Hard limits & enforcement
- The code includes collection-level limits (example constants): MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION = 100_000, plus limits on collections/docs that can affect how much content is considered or allowed.

Additional notes
- The implementation is integrated into the MCP server as rlm_context_query (new tool) and uses token-aware greedy selection + smart truncation to optimize context while respecting the token budget.
- The tool also returns timing/usage metadata (e.g., search_time_ms, sections_scanned) in its usage/ToolResult wrapper.

Summary of how Snipara’s token budgeting works (facts from the documentation):

Main idea
- Context is selected to fit a caller-specified token budget (rlm_context_query’s max_tokens, default 4000) by counting tokens for candidate document sections and filling the budget in priority order until exhausted.
- Token counting uses tiktoken (cl100k_base encoder); tiktoken is added to the MCP server (pyproject requirement >=0.5.0).

Context selection algorithm (rlm_context_query)
- Parameters: query (string), max_tokens (int, default 4000), search_mode ("keyword" | "semantic" | "hybrid"), include_metadata (bool).
- Search modes: keyword, semantic, or hybrid (fuse keyword + semantic results).
- Candidates are ranked by relevance score.
- Greedy selection: iterate highest-relevance sections first, add a section if its token_count fits into remaining budget.
- If a section would overflow the remaining budget but some tokens remain, a partial section is added using smart_truncate (see below) and the budget is filled; selection then stops.
- Response includes per-section metadata: title, content (possibly truncated), file path, line range, relevance_score, token_count; plus overall fields: total_tokens, truncated (whether the overall response hit the budget), session_context, suggestions.

Smart truncation
- Implemented to truncate partial sections to fit remaining tokens.
- Truncation is done at sentence boundaries (cut on . ! ?), not mid-sentence.
- Truncated content is marked (truncated: true) and an ellipsis ("...") indicator is appended.

Session & system handling
- Session context can be included and is accounted for in budgeting (documentation notes session context inclusion up to 20% of the budget).
- System instructions may be auto-injected and are considered in context building.

Allocation & budgeting strategy (category-based and project priority)
- There are predefined allocation defaults and priorities for categories. Two places in the docs show example allocations:
  - Token Budget Algorithm constants (example): MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10% (category priorities assigned as well).
  - Web service DEFAULT_ALLOCATION example: project: 40%, MANDATORY: 25%, BEST_PRACTICES: 20%, GUIDELINES: 10%, REFERENCE: 5% (project-specific docs are given priority).
- The allocation code groups docs (project vs shared categories), computes per-category budgets (fraction of totalBudget), and then allocates within each bucket sorted by relevance until that bucket’s tokens are used.
- Hard limits exist (examples in code): MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION, MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION, etc.; collections exceeding limits are truncated to fit.

Outputs & metadata
- The rlm_context_query response structure (documented example) returns selected sections plus total_tokens, truncated flag, session_context, and suggestions. Per-section metadata includes file and line ranges, relevance scores, and token counts.

Where this is implemented / documented
- rlm_context_query and its token-budgeting behavior are described in the "4.1 Token Budgeting & Metadata Response" and "Context Query Implementation" sections; example implementations and allocation logic are shown in the docs and example files (rlm_engine.py pseudocode, token-budget.ts, and the DEFAULT_ALLOCATION examples).

Summary — what they are
- Shared context collections are team-owned collections of reusable instructions (coding standards, best practices, guidelines, reference material) that are linked to projects so every project/query can automatically include the team's rules and conventions.
- Purpose: enforce consistent, team-wide behavior (security, architecture, code style, review templates, etc.) across all team projects.

How they work (high-level workflow)
1. Team admin creates a Shared Context Collection and uploads documents (e.g., with rlm_upload_shared_document).
2. Documents are categorized (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE) and given a priority (0–100) within the category.
3. Collections are linked to projects (via the dashboard). Projects automatically inherit all documents from linked collections.
4. When you query project context (rlm_context_query), the shared context is merged into the results first, then local project docs are searched and returned. Shared context can also be fetched directly with rlm_shared_context.
5. Teams can inject the returned shared context into session context so subsequent queries and completions follow the standards automatically.

Key details and behavior
- Categories & priority (document-level organization)
  - MANDATORY — highest priority (non-negotiable rules like security/architecture)
  - BEST_PRACTICES — recommended patterns (code style, testing)
  - GUIDELINES — helpful suggestions (naming, structure)
  - REFERENCE — background info and examples
- Token/budget allocation
  - Within shared context, documentation shows a category budget breakdown commonly used: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%.
  - When running a project query, shared context is included first and there is an overall split between shared-context tokens and project tokens. Examples in the docs show 40% shared / 60% project and other examples show a configurable shared_context_budget_percent (example default/usage: 30%). The shared vs. project allocation can be controlled via rlm_context_query parameters (include_shared_context, shared_context_budget_percent).
- Tools and APIs mentioned
  - rlm_list_collections (list available collections)
  - rlm_upload_shared_document (upload documents into a collection)
  - rlm_shared_context (get merged shared context from linked collections)
  - rlm_context_query (when querying project docs, shared context is merged/included first)
  - rlm_list_templates / rlm_get_template (list/get templates stored in collections)
- Access / plan
  - Shared collections and team-wide features require the Team plan (or Enterprise). Not available on Free/Pro according to the docs.

Common usage patterns
- Load team standards at session start with rlm_shared_context, inject into session context, then run rlm_context_query or completions so outputs follow the team rules (e.g., “MUST follow MANDATORY rules”).
- Use categories and priority to control what is enforced vs. recommended and to influence how much of the shared content is returned.

Short answer
- Shared context collections are team-managed collections of reusable instructions (coding standards, best practices, architecture rules, templates, reference docs) that are automatically made available to all projects linked to the collection.
- They’re used to enforce team conventions by merging those documents into every relevant query so AI-assisted work follows team rules.

How they work (workflow)
1. Admin creates a Shared Context Collection (Team plan required).
2. Admin uploads documents (e.g., coding standards) to the collection (example API: rlm_upload_shared_document).
3. The collection is linked to projects in the dashboard (Project → Shared Context → Link Collection).
4. When you run queries, the shared context is merged with project docs and returned first so team rules are applied before project-specific content.
5. You can fetch the merged team context programmatically with rlm_shared_context and then inject it into a session (rlm_inject) so subsequent queries include those standards.

Key behaviors and controls
- Categories and priority: documents are categorized and prioritized. Categories (in priority order) are:
  - MANDATORY — non‑negotiable rules (highest priority)
  - BEST_PRACTICES — recommended patterns
  - GUIDELINES — suggestions
  - REFERENCE — background/examples
- Token-budget allocation: the system allocates token budget across categories (example allocation: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%). When merging shared context with project docs, shared context is queried first and a share of the query budget is reserved for it. rlm_context_query supports parameters such as include_shared_context and shared_context_budget_percent to control how much budget shared context receives.
- In practice you fetch shared context with rlm_shared_context (you can filter categories and set max_tokens), then use rlm_context_query or inject the shared context into the session so all subsequent queries honor team rules.

APIs / tools referenced in docs
- rlm_list_collections (discover collections)
- rlm_upload_shared_document (add documents to a collection)
- rlm_shared_context (get merged context from linked collections)
- rlm_context_query (project queries that automatically include shared context)
- rlm_list_templates, rlm_get_template (prompt templates shipped in collections)
- rlm_multi_project_query (search across team projects)

Access / limits
- Shared context collections and auto-injection are Team- or Enterprise-level features (not available on Free/Pro plans).
- Collections are linked to projects once (via dashboard) so projects automatically inherit documents.

Best-practice notes from the docs
- Put non-negotiable rules in MANDATORY, add BEST_PRACTICES gradually, keep documents focused (one topic per document), and use priority fields (0–100) to indicate importance within a category.

Short answer
- Snipara implements the OAuth Device Flow (RFC 8628) so users can authenticate from a CLI without copying API keys: run a CLI command, open a browser URL, enter (or follow) a short user code, and complete authorization in the browser.

How the flow works (documented endpoints / behavior)
1. Client requests a device code
   - Endpoint: POST /api/oauth/device/code
   - Request body (example): { "client_id": "snipara_cli" } (the docs also show an example using an optional auto_provision flag)
   - Response includes:
     - device_code ( opaque code for the client )
     - user_code (e.g., "ABCD-1234") — code the user enters
     - verification_uri (e.g., "https://snipara.com/device") — page the user visits
     - verification_uri_complete (e.g., "https://snipara.com/device?code=ABCD-1234") — direct link including code
     - expires_in (seconds until the device_code expires, e.g., 900)
     - interval (recommended polling interval, e.g., 5)

2. User authorizes in browser
   - The user opens verification_uri (or the verification_uri_complete link) and enters the user_code (or follows the complete link).
   - There is a GET /api/oauth/device/authorize page that shows the device authorization page (accepts query param code).

3. Client polls / exchanges for a token
   - The client uses the returned device_code and polls according to interval until the user completes authorization, then receives an OAuth access token (the docs reference RFC 8628 behavior in the flow).

What the token is used for / precedence
- OAuth tokens are sent as: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...

Summary (from Snipara docs)

- Snipara implements the OAuth Device Flow (RFC 8628) so users can authenticate without copying API keys: they run a CLI command, open a browser to a verification URL, enter a code, and authenticate there.

How the flow is started
- Client calls POST /api/oauth/device/code with a body like:
  {"client_id": "snipara_cli"}

What the server returns
- The endpoint responds with the device authorization details:
  - device_code (string)
  - user_code (string)
  - verification_uri (URL where the user should go)
  - verification_uri_complete (URL that already includes the code)
  - expires_in (seconds until the code expires)
  - interval (recommended polling interval in seconds)

Where tokens are stored and priority of auth
- OAuth access tokens are stored in ~/.snipara/tokens.json.
- MCP server authentication priority: check for a stored OAuth token first, then fall back to an API key from environment variables.

Request header formats
- OAuth token: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
- API key: X-API-Key: rlm_...

Troubleshooting & alternatives (from docs)
- If OAuth has expired, re-run the device flow to re-authenticate.
- To force API-key authentication instead of OAuth, either:
  - Set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the client environment (client will skip ~/.snipara/tokens.json and use SNIPARA_API_KEY), or
  - Remove or move ~/.snipara/tokens.json (e.g., mv ~/.snipara/tokens.json ~/.snipara/tokens.json.bak) to force fallback to API key.

Snipara uses a strict layered architecture (top → bottom) with clear responsibilities and import rules:

- Layers (top to bottom)
  - Routes — app/api/ (thin HTTP handlers)
  - Services — lib/services/ (external API adapters / business integrations)
  - Repositories — lib/db/queries/ (centralized CRUD / DB access)
  - Utils & Types — types/, utils/ (shared, no dependencies)

- Import rule
  - Lower layers can import from lower (downwards) but cannot import from higher layers. types/ and utils/ must have no dependencies on other layers.

- Key patterns / responsibilities
  - Route handlers are thin: authenticate (requireAuth), validate input (Zod), delegate to a service/query, return a response (try/catch + handleError).
  - Repository pattern: all DB queries live in lib/db/queries/ (never call Prisma directly in route handlers); repository functions include permission checks.
  - Services pattern: external API adapters live in lib/services/, use lazy initialization and retry for clients (example: Stripe adapter).

These points are taken directly from the project documentation.

Snipara uses a strict layered (vertical) architecture with a single import direction and clear responsibilities:

- Layers (top → bottom / may import downward):
  - Routes (apps/web/src/app/api/): thin HTTP route handlers — authenticate, validate (Zod), and delegate to services or queries.
  - Services (lib/services/): external API adapters and business logic that call repositories.
  - Repositories (lib/db/queries/): centralized CRUD / DB access (repository pattern). Route handlers must not call Prisma directly — all DB access goes through these queries.
  - Utils & Types (types/, lib/utils/): shared pure utilities and type definitions.

- Rules enforced by the pattern (documented):
  - Lower layers cannot import from higher layers (import hierarchy is one-way).
  - types/ and utils/ have no dependencies on other layers.
  - Database queries must live only in lib/db/queries/.
  - Route handlers must be thin and follow the documented pattern (requireAuth → validate → delegate → return response).

(These points are taken directly from the project’s TypeScript Architecture Layers / Layer Hierarchy documentation.)

Short answer
- Snipara reduces a 500K‑token codebase to a small, relevant context (the docs state “90% Context Reduction: From 500K tokens to ~5K relevant tokens”), so the LLM receives only the most relevant text (well under a 128K context window).
- For even larger/complex queries Snipara can decompose and orchestrate recursive queries (rlm_decompose + rlm_multi_query) so a 500K+ codebase can be handled even with much smaller LLM windows (the docs show this approach handling 500K+ docs with a 4K window).

How Snipara achieves this (documented facts)
- rlm_context_query: token budgeting (client sets max_tokens), greedy selection of highest‑relevance sections, accurate token counting (tiktoken cl100k_base), and smart truncation at sentence boundaries. Returned metadata includes file path, line ranges, relevance scores, and token counts.
- Session & shared context: session context can persist and Snipara can include team shared context first (you can allocate a percent of the token budget to shared context via parameters like shared_context_budget_percent).
- Recursive tools: rlm_decompose, rlm_multi_query, rlm_plan let Snipara break a large query into subqueries and run per-subquery context collection so the client LLM can synthesize results — enabling “near‑infinite” effective context.
- LLM-agnostic and no inference costs from Snipara: Snipara does not run the LLM itself — the client uses their own LLM (so you don’t pay Snipara for model inference).
- Zero setup & team collaboration: hosted endpoint, shared indexed documentation, session persistence.

- Token traffic reduction: instead of sending huge amounts of document tokens to your LLM, Snipara returns a compact, high‑relevance subset (example: ~5K tokens). That directly reduces the number of input tokens you send to your LLM, so you pay less to the LLM provider for context tokens.
- You pay Snipara according to plan (examples in the docs): FREE $0 (100 queries/mo); PRO $19/mo (5,000 queries/mo); TEAM $49/mo (20,000 queries/mo); ENTERPRISE $499/mo (Unlimited). Snipara’s cost model is per‑plan (queries/month), not per‑token inference, because Snipara does not run the LLM.
- Recursive approach avoids needing to expand your LLM context window (so you don’t need a larger/more expensive model solely for larger context).

Available authentication options (from the docs) and how they differ in security:

1) Project API Key
- Header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
- Use case: single-project access (used by both MCP and Inject endpoints; Inject requires X-API-Key).
- Implementation / security notes (doc-backed):
  - Keys use the rlm_ prefix.
  - The server hashes the key with SHA-256 and stores/looks up the hash in the ApiKey table (so raw keys are not stored in DB).
  - When displayed in UI/logs only the first 8 chars are shown.
  - Validation prefers a project-specific key first, then falls back to a team key.
- Implication from docs: key material is protected at rest by hashing in DB and limited display in logs/UI.

2) Team API Key
- Header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
- Use case: access across all projects in a team (same header/format as project key).
- Implementation / security notes: same storage/lookup behavior as project keys (SHA-256 hash lookup). Different scope of access (team-wide vs single-project).

3) OAuth access token (Device Flow / MCP Authentication)
- Header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
- Use case: Device-flow OAuth (RFC 8628) for MCP authentication so users can authenticate via browser without copying API keys.
- Implementation / security notes (doc-backed):
  - Token format uses the snipara_at_ prefix.
  - Tokens are validated via DB lookup and expiry checks (there is a validate_oauth_token()).
  - Scopes are enforced (read/write scope enforcement is implemented).
  - Device flow endpoints exist (/api/auth/device/ and POST /api/oauth/device/token) and tokens can be polled during the device flow.
  - Tokens can be listed (GET /api/settings/tokens) and revoked (DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[tokenId]).
  - If a token is expired or revoked, requests return an “Invalid or expired OAuth token” 401 and the docs recommend re-authenticating.
- Implication from docs: OAuth tokens are time-limited, scope-limited, and revocable — the device flow avoids copying API keys into the CLI/browser.

Other operational details from the docs
- MCP server accepts both auth types; priority is: check stored OAuth token in ~/.snipara/tokens.json first, then fall back to API key from environment variables.
- Inject API endpoint requires X-API-Key (API key only).
- The docs include a troubleshooting tip: removing ~/.snipara/tokens.json will force the client to use API key authentication instead of a stored OAuth token.

Summary of security differences (documented facts)
- API keys: rlm_ prefix, hashed with SHA-256 in DB (protects stored key material), displayed partially in UI/logs, long-lived unless rotated/revoked in dashboard (docs advise generating new key if “Invalid API key”).
- OAuth tokens: snipara_at_ prefix, validated with DB + expiry checks, scope enforcement, issued via device flow (avoids copying API key), listable and revocable via API — designed for time-limited, scoped access.

1) Project API Key
- How to send: X-API-Key: rlm_...  
- Format/DB storage: keys use an rlm_ prefix and are SHA‑256 hashed in the database (validate_api_key()).  
- Lookup behavior: server hashes the presented key and looks up the hash (project-specific keys tried first, then team keys).  
- Visibility/logging: prefix-only shown in logs; UI/logs display only the first 8 chars.  
- Where accepted: both MCP and Inject endpoints accept X-API-Key (MCP accepts X-API-Key; Inject requires X-API-Key).  
- Security notes from docs: hashing in DB provides at-rest protection for stored keys; key-scoping is project vs team (project-first, then team fallback).

2) Team API Key
- Same header/format as project keys (X-API-Key: rlm_...).  
- Managed by team owners with API for listing/creating/revoking team keys.  
- Security differences vs project key: team keys grant access across team projects (broader scope), so revocation and management endpoints exist.

3) OAuth access tokens (Bearer)
- How to send: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...  
- Token format: access tokens have a snipara_at_ prefix; refresh tokens present in device/token responses.  
- Validation: validated by DB lookup plus expiry check; scopes enforced (validate_oauth_token()).  
- Lifecycle: tokens have expiry (expires_in shown in device flow response) and can be revoked. Endpoints: device flow endpoints (/api/oauth/device/*), /api/oauth/revoke, and user token management GET /api/settings/tokens and DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[id].  
- Security notes from docs: tokens are time-limited and revocable; validation checks expiry and scopes.

4) OAuth Device Flow (RFC 8628) — how you obtain OAuth tokens
- Flow: CLI issues device-code, user authenticates in browser, CLI polls /api/oauth/device/token for tokens.  
- Security note in docs: described as enabling secure MCP authentication without copying API keys (i.e., avoids exposing API keys during login).

5) Host/endpoint specific behavior / precedence
- MCP server: accepts either OAuth Bearer or X-API-Key; the client/server logic checks for a stored OAuth token (~/.snipara/tokens.json) first and falls back to API key from environment if no OAuth token is found. (Docs include an explicit precedence: check tokens.json then env var.)  
- Inject API: documented as accepting only X-API-Key (REST inject endpoint expects X-API-Key).

Documented security differences (summary)
- Storage at rest: API keys are stored hashed (SHA‑256) in DB — reduces risk if DB leaks.  
- Token lifecycle: OAuth tokens are time-limited (expires_in) and revocable; they include refresh tokens for renewal.  
- Scope/Granularity: API keys are project or team scoped (project-first lookup, team fallback); OAuth tokens have enforced scopes.  
- Operational security: Device flow avoids copying API keys in manual steps (explicitly noted as “secure” for MCP authentication).  
- Endpoint constraints: Inject API requires an API key (so OAuth tokens can’t be used there per docs); MCP accepts either, so OAuth tokens (if present) take precedence on the client.

Additional operational facts from docs
- You can list and revoke OAuth tokens via GET /api/settings/tokens and DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[id].  
- If an OAuth token is invalid or expired the server returns a 401 and message to re-authenticate (docs show the standard message).  
- Troubleshooting notes for API keys include regenerating keys when “Invalid API key” occurs.

Short answer — the docs show two primary auth methods, with variants and different security properties:

1) API keys
- Variants: project API keys (project-scoped) and team API keys (team-scoped, broader access / one key for many projects).
- Header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
- Where accepted: MCP server and Inject API (Inject API uses X-API-Key only; MCP accepts X-API-Key or Bearer).
- Storage & validation: clients present the raw key; server hashes the key (SHA‑256) and compares the hash to the DB (auth.py: validate_api_key). Only the key prefix is shown in UI/logs.
- Lifespan & revocation: keys are shown once at creation, can include an expiresAt when created, and can be revoked from the dashboard (project/team API key endpoints exist).
- Security implications (from docs):
  - Keys are stored hashed in DB (SHA‑256) — protects raw value on the server side.
  - Team keys are less granular (broader access) than project keys — higher blast radius if leaked.
  - Keys must be kept secret (revocation and regeneration are the recovery path).

2) OAuth tokens (Device Flow)
- Mechanism: OAuth device flow (RFC 8628). CLI triggers device flow → user authenticates in browser → server issues access + refresh tokens.
- Token format & header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
- Where accepted: MCP server accepts Bearer OAuth tokens (MCP supports both OAuth and API key).
- Storage & validation: tokens are tracked in the tokens API (GET /api/settings/tokens lists tokens) and validated by DB lookup + expiry check; tokens have expiry (example access_token response includes expires_in and a refresh_token).
- Lifespan & revocation: tokens expire and can be revoked (DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[tokenId] returns success).
- Security implications (from docs):
  - OAuth tokens have expiry and refresh tokens — limits exposure window compared to an unexpired, long-lived secret key.
  - Scopes are enforced (read/write scope enforcement is mentioned) — finer-grained permissions.
  - Device flow avoids copying API keys (explicitly described as “enables secure MCP authentication without copying API keys”).
  - Note: the client stores OAuth tokens locally (docs reference ~/.snipara/tokens.json as the stored OAuth token location and that removing it forces API key use) — local storage is a practical security consideration.

Other operational notes from the docs
- Authentication precedence: the MCP client first checks for a stored OAuth token (~/.snipara/tokens.json) and falls back to API key from environment variables if no valid OAuth token is present.
- Endpoint differences: Inject API only accepts X-API-Key; MCP accepts both X-API-Key and Authorization: Bearer.
- Error handling: expired/revoked OAuth tokens return a 401 with guidance to re-authenticate; invalid API keys return “Invalid API key” and require regeneration/checking.

Short answer
- When you query, shared context is merged with project docs and a token budget is split between them; shared context is loaded first, then local project docs use the remaining budget.
- Within the shared context, tokens are allocated to document categories (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE) according to configured percentages, and documents are selected by relevance until each category’s budget is exhausted.

Details and exact behavior (facts from the docs)
- Query order: rlm_context_query loads shared best-practices first, then queries local project scope with the remaining token budget (MCP Query Order).
- Shared vs project split: the docs show this is configurable and that different examples use different defaults:
  - MCP Query Order shows a default of 30% shared / 70% project and documents the parameter shared_context_budget_percent to override it.
  - Another example (rlm_context_query usage) shows token budget distributed as 60% project + 40% shared.
  - rlm_shared_context’s example response shows a budget_allocation for shared categories (see below).
- Shared-category percentages (examples in docs):
  - Document Categories table and the rlm_shared_context example use: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%.
  - Note: the code in apps/web/lib/services/token-budget.ts defines a DEFAULT_ALLOCATION used by allocateTokenBudget that differs (project: 40%, MANDATORY: 25%, BEST_PRACTICES: 20%, GUIDELINES: 10%, REFERENCE: 5%). Both are present in the documentation.
- How allocation runs in code (allocateTokenBudget):
  - A totalBudget is split using an allocation map (DEFAULT_ALLOCATION by default; can be overridden).
  - Project docs are allocated first: projectBudget = floor(totalBudget * allocation.project).
  - Shared docs are grouped by category and each category is given floor(totalBudget * allocation[category]).
  - Within each category, documents are sorted by relevanceScore (if present) and included until that category’s token budget is used. Documents that don’t fit are marked allocated: false with allocationReason 'exceeded budget'.
- Configurability:
  - rlm_context_query and related calls accept parameters to control max_tokens and shared_context_budget_percent / categories, and rlm_shared_context accepts category filtering and max_tokens.
- Hard limits:
  - There are collection-level and system limits enforced (e.g., MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION = 100_000, MAX_COLLECTIONS_PER_PROJECT = 10). enforceCollectionLimits truncates collections to fit the per-collection token cap.

Summary of how token budget allocation works (facts from the docs)

- Shared context + project docs share the query token budget. Shared context is loaded first and then project docs are queried with the remaining budget (MCP Query Order).

- Category-level allocations (examples in docs / rlm_shared_context):

- Code-level default allocation (apps/web/lib/services/token-budget.ts):
  - DEFAULT_ALLOCATION in code is: project: 0.40, MANDATORY: 0.25, BEST_PRACTICES: 0.20, GUIDELINES: 0.10, REFERENCE: 0.05.
  - allocateTokenBudget(doc[], totalBudget, ...) implements the allocation:
    1. Compute projectBudget = floor(totalBudget * allocation.project) and allocate project docs first.
    2. For each shared-category (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE) compute categoryBudget = floor(totalBudget * allocation[category]) and allocate within that budget.
    3. allocateWithinBudget sorts docs by relevanceScore (if present) and then includes documents until tokensUsed + doc.tokenCount would exceed the category/project budget. Included docs are marked allocated:true (with allocationReason); others are marked allocated:false with allocationReason 'exceeded budget'.

- Query parameters and examples:
  - rlm_context_query / MCP examples show a parameter shared_context_budget_percent (example value 30) and an example where shared context consumes 30% (shared first) and project gets the remaining 70%.
  - Other examples show different splits (e.g., a separate example says token budget is distributed 60% project / 40% shared). These examples are present in the docs; the actual split is determined by the allocation configuration/parameters or defaults in code.

- Hard limits enforced:
  - LIMITS in code: MAX_COLLECTIONS_PER_PROJECT = 10, MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION = 100_000, MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION = 50, MIN_RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD = 0.2.
  - enforceCollectionLimits truncates a collection’s documents to respect MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION.

Takeaway
- The system divides the total token budget between project docs and shared context, then further splits shared context across categories (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE). The allocation logic is implemented in allocateTokenBudget (project docs allocated first, then shared categories), and individual docs are chosen by relevance until each category’s budget is exhausted. The exact percentages shown vary across examples and the code; the code’s DEFAULT_ALLOCATION and the rlm_shared_context examples in the docs are the authoritative sources to consult for the current defaults.

When a user exceeds their monthly query limit the API returns a 429 Too Many Requests response with rate-limit details. Example (from the docs):

{
  "success": false,
  "error": "Monthly query limit exceeded",
  "usage": {
    "current": 101,
    "max": 100,
    "exceeded": true,
    "reset_at": "2026-02-01T00:00:00Z"
  }
}

Fields:
- current (int): Current usage count this billing period  
- max (int): Maximum allowed queries (-1 means unlimited)  
- exceeded (boolean): Whether the limit has been reached  
- reset_at (string): ISO 8601 timestamp when the monthly limit resets

(The docs also include an alternate error example for query-limit failures that returns an "INSUFFICIENT_CREDITS" error object.)

From the docs:

- Monthly query limit exceeded
  - The API returns a 429 Too Many Requests response with rate-limit details. Example:
    {
      "success": false,
      "error": "Monthly query limit exceeded",
      "usage": {
        "current": 101,
        "max": 100,
        "exceeded": true,
        "reset_at": "2026-02-01T00:00:00Z"
      }
    }
  - The usage object fields are: current (current count), max (allowed queries; -1 = unlimited), exceeded (boolean), reset_at (ISO 8601 timestamp when the monthly limit resets).
  - In other documented query-limit responses you may see an error with code "INSUFFICIENT_CREDITS" (e.g. {"error":{"code":"INSUFFICIENT_CREDITS","message":"Need 5000 credits, have 5000"}}).

- Per-minute rate limit exceeded
  - The API may return a 429 with an error like:
    {"error":{"code":"RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED","message":"Rate limit exceeded. Try again in 60 seconds"}}

- Rate-limit headers
  - All MCP API responses include X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, and X-RateLimit-Reset to help clients monitor remaining quota and reset timing.

Short answer — documented behavior:

- rlm_context_query will return a normal response even if nothing matches: the handler builds a response object with "sections" equal to the selected_sections list (which can be empty) and a token count variable that will remain 0. Concretely the implementation returns:
  - "sections": [] (empty),
  - "total_tokens": 0,
  - "truncated": false (since total_tokens < max_tokens),
  - and a "suggestions" field produced by _generate_suggestions(query, selected_sections).
  (See the Context Query Implementation code in docs.)

- The API can also return an explicit 404 error when no context is found. The docs include a 404 example payload:
  {
    "error": {
      "code": "NOT_FOUND",
      "message": "Context not found"
    }
  }

- UI guidance for empty-results states is documented: show a helpful message when there is no data, use padding (py-8) rather than fixed heights, and include action buttons where appropriate.
